front: Work: Nathanael falls in love with the automaton Olympia created by Coppelius/Spalanzani, who steals children's eyes, in this story
Work: Freud's essay 'The Uncanny' analyzes this story, arguing that castration anxiety underlies the eye-stealing motif
Work: the barometer/telescope salesman Coppola advertises 'pretty eyes, pretty eyes' in this story
Work: in this story, the protagonist shrieks 'Circle of fire! Spin round!' while holding Clara over a ledge, then jumps from a bell-tower to his death
Work: this story's protagonist calls his fiancee Clara 'accursed, inanimate' after she asks him to burn a 'frightful' poem
Work: this story inspired Leo Delibes's ballet Coppelia when combined with 'The Doll'
Work: the barcarolle 'Belle nuit, o nuit d'amour' opens the Venetian act of this Offenbach opera
Work: the 'Doll Song' ('Les oiseaux dans la charmille') is sung by the automaton Olympia, who must be periodically rewound, in this opera
Work: in this opera, three loves — Olympia, Antonia, Giulietta — are facets of the prima donna Stella
Work: in this opera, four villains are sung by one bass-baritone; Dapertutto offers a diamond to steal the protagonist's reflection
Work: in this opera, Nicklausse reveals himself to be the Muse at the end
Work: the 'Legend of Kleinzach' describes a hunchbacked dwarf but the singer gets distracted by thoughts of a woman in this opera
Work: Dr. Miracle kills Antonia by making a portrait of her dead mother urge her to sing in this opera
Work: the clockmaker Drosselmeier creates a toy that defends Marie/Clara from a seven-headed Mouse King in this fairy tale
back: The Nutcracker and the Mouse King (E. T. A. Hoffmann)
work: The Nutcracker and the Mouse King

front: Work: a printer's error splices an intelligent cat's autobiography with pages from a biography of Kapellmeister Kreisler in this novel
back: The Life and Opinions of Tomcat Murr (E. T. A. Hoffmann)
work: The Life and Opinions of Tomcat Murr

front: Work: the student Anselmus spills ink on Archivist Lindhorst's manuscripts and is imprisoned in a crystal bottle in this fairy tale
Work: this novella, often cited alongside Poe as one of the first detective stories, follows the title character solving the mystery of goldsmith Rene Cardillac's death
Work: the monk Medardus is driven mad by the title concoction and murders Princess Aurelie's family before stabbing her on their wedding day in this novel
back: The Devil's Elixirs (E. T. A. Hoffmann)

front: Author: this author's music criticism, collected as Kreisleriana, declared 'Music is the most romantic of all the arts' and praised Beethoven as 'purely romantic'
Author: this German Romantic author changed his middle name from Wilhelm to 'Amadeus' in honor of Mozart
Work: in this story, Erasmus Spikher barters away his reflection, alluding to Chamisso's Peter Schlemihl
back: "A New Year's Eve Adventure" (E. T. A. Hoffmann)

front: Work: in this story, a strange man from the Tiergarten sings an aria from blank pages then claims to be a dead composer
Work: in this Hoffmann story, Fet-Mats is discovered in a mine; Ziolkowski argues it uses the mine as 'an image of the soul'
back: "The Mines of Falun" (E. T. A. Hoffmann)

front: Work: the title Hoffmann character forbids his daughter Antonia from singing because she risks death; inspired Act II of The Tales of Hoffmann
